title: Kaitiaki o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku | Guardians of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: Students will explore their role as kaitiaki of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our college lands. This awa holds significance in Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau as the lifeblood of our whenua, with traditional stories telling of its healing properties and role in sustaining community wellbeing. Students will connect with local kaumātua to understand the historical and spiritual relationship between our people and this waterway.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain three ways Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku contributes to community hauora (physical, mental, social, and spiritual wellbeing)
Design and implement a practical water quality monitoring activity using both mātauranga Māori and scientific methods
Create a personal kaitiakitanga action plan demonstrating commitment to protecting and enhancing the health of our local awa
Collaborate effectively in small groups to assess environmental challenges and propose culturally-grounded solutions

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa
activities.description: Students gather at Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua for karakia and introduction to the pūrākau of the waterway. Through guided storytelling, students learn about the historical significance of the awa and its role in traditional healing practices, food gathering, and spiritual connection.

activities.title: Mauri Meter Investigation
activities.description: Using both traditional assessment methods (observing wildlife presence, water clarity, plant health) and scientific tools (pH testing kits, temperature gauges), students conduct a comprehensive health check of the awa. They document findings using a 'Mauri Meter' scale that combines mātauranga Māori and scientific indicators.

activities.title: Hauora Mapping
activities.description: Students create detailed maps showing the connection between the awa's health and community wellbeing. They identify areas where the stream supports physical activity (swimming spots), mental wellbeing (quiet contemplation areas), social connection (gathering places), and spiritual significance (historical sites).

activities.title: Kaitiaki Action Planning
activities.description: Working in small groups, students develop practical action plans to address one identified issue affecting the awa's health. Plans must include both modern conservation approaches and traditional practices, such as planting native rongoā plants along banks or organizing community clean-up events with cultural protocols.

activities.title: Whakapapa of Water
activities.description: Students trace the journey of water from our local maunga to the sea, understanding how their kaitiakitanga actions contribute to the larger ecosystem. They create visual representations showing the interconnectedness of all water systems and their responsibility as upstream kaitiaki.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Hone Smith for pūrākau and cultural guidance
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water testing kits and monitoring guides
Te Ara Encyclopedia entry 'Traditional Māori Healing and Rongoā'
Local conservation group 'Friends of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku' field experts

tasks: Completed Mauri Meter assessment with both cultural and scientific indicators
Personal kaitiakitanga action plan with measurable goals and timeline
Group presentation on proposed conservation solution with cultural justification
Reflective journal documenting personal connection to the awa throughout the unit

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A3 - Safety management; B1 - Personal identity; C1 - Relationships; D1 - Community resources
Social Sciences: 1.2 - Understand how people make choices to meet needs/wants; 2.1 - Understand how cultural practices reflect expressions of identity
Science: Nature of Science - Participating and contributing; Living World - Ecology
